data.title: AI-related stocks edge higher, led by networking, while CoreWeave drags hyperscalers down
data.description:
Artificial intelligence-related stocks, along with the broader market, trended up during Friday trading after the U.S. Supreme Court's decision to strike down President Donald Trump's tariffs.

Several networking stocks, particularly those focused on optical products, were showing the strongest gains. Lumentum (LITE) and Ciena (CIEN) had both increased by 6%. Corning (GLW) and Coherent (COHR) had both jumped 7%. Applied Optoelectronics (AAOI) had popped 11%.

Applied Optoelectronics is in the process of building a 210,000-square-foot manufacturing facility in Sugar Land, Texas.

CoreWeave drags down hyperscalers

Several of the most popular hyperscaler stocks were not responding positively during Friday trading. Oracle (ORCL), Nebius (NBIS), and Applied Digital (APLD) had all declined by about 4%. IREN (IREN) had inched down 0.6%. CoreWeave (CRWV) had plunged 9%.

CoreWeave is facing a class action lawsuit over alleged data center delays and concealed infrastructure risks. The suit is being led by the law firm Hagens Berman, which specializes in shareholder rights.

"We are investigating the alleged gap between the company's assurances of its growth trajectory and the alleged reality of systemic construction delays at its primary data center sites," said Reed Kathrein, the Hagens Berman partner leading the firm's investigation.

Chipmakers show mixed action

The most significant names among chipmakers were demonstrating mixed action. AMD (AMD) was down 0.6%, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) perked up 2%, Intel (INTC) dipped 3%, and Nvidia (NVDA) inched up 0.4%.

The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX) had increased 1%, surpassing the larger indices in daily gains. The S&P 500 Index (SP500) had ticked up 0.5%, the NASDAQ Composite Index (COMP:IND) edged up 0.8%, and the Dow Jones Industrial Average Index (DJI) was up 0.2%.

Meanwhile, Rackspace (RXT) continued the surge it started earlier this week after partnering with Palantir (PLTR) to help increase the speed at which enterprises can deploy and operate its analytical and AI-driven software. It had soared more than 40% by noon trading, and its stock price has now more than quadrupled since Monday.

data.title: Compass stock slips after Sen. Warren questions DOJ review of Anywhere deal
data.description:
Compass (COMP) stock fell 2.4% in Friday midday trading following a report that Senator Elizabeth Warren (D-MA) questioned the Justice Department about its review of Compass's Anywhere Real Estate acquisition.

The transaction was completed in January, less than four months after the $10B all-stock merger agreement was announced. On the same day that the deal closed, the Wall Street Journal reported that the head of the DOJ's antitrust division wanted an extended review of the real estate brokerage deal but was overruled by senior officials. 

Bloomberg News reported on Friday that Sen. Warren wrote a letter to U.S. Attorney General Pam Bondi seeking more information about the review.

Sen. Warren's office didn't immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Lucid Group to lay off 12% of workforce -- report
data.description:
Lucid Motors (LCID) is reportedly cutting 12% of its workforce to improve profitability even as the company increases production of its Gravity SUV and plans to launch a $50K mid-sized EV later this year. 

According to an internal memo viewed by TechCrunch, California-based Lucid (LCID) said the job cuts aim to “improve operational effectiveness and optimize our resources as we continue on our path toward profitability.”

While the cuts could impact more than 800 Lucid (LCID) employees, those working in manufacturing, logistics, and quality are reportedly not affected.

The company said it will provide severance, bonuses, continued health benefits, and transition services to support those employees who will be laid off. Costs associated with the separations were not disclosed. 

Lucid (LCID) shares are down again on Friday, extending its losing streak to a fourth straight day and adding to its 70% year-over-year loss.

data.title: Celestica's growth, engineering capabilities should widen its opportunities: BNP
data.description:
Celestica (CLS) shares have been hit hard recently amid the continued perceived artificial intelligence-induced disruption. However, BNP Paribas believes the company's growth and engineering prowess could be a buying opportunity for investors.

“We believe Celestica's growing capacity and engineering capabilities widens the company's [Original Design Manufacturer] networking opportunity and margin expansion potential,” BNP analyst Karl Ackerman wrote in a note to clients after a meeting with the company's CFO and investor relations team. “CLS remains a top idea.”

Delving deeper, Ackerman said the company's original design manufacturing capabilities likely helped it see customer orders in 2025 that were “multiples” ahead of where they have been in prior years. As such, there is the belief that 2026 could see a similar response. 

Additionally, Ackerman said that new orders typically take around three years to turn into material sales, so the $1B in capex spending this year should help boost growth in 2027, 2028, and 2029. He also said he believes Celestica has won two follow-on programs for Google's (GOOG) (GOOGL) tensor processing units, which should start to ramp up in 2027, notable given that Google could see external demand for its TPUs.

Other potential catalysts include the 1.6T networking switch programs across the three major hyperscalers that will start to hit in the second-half of 2026 and throughout 2027; its ability to compete in co-packaged optics for switch opportunities; the company's Digital Native program, which is on track to ramp in the first-half of 2027; and the fact it is likely engaged with multiple Ethernet for Scale-Up Networking opportunities. 

“We see multiple tailwinds to support 40%+ revenue growth and margin expansion through CY28 for Celestica, such as ramping server and switch programs at hyperscalers, broadening opportunities with digital natives, a modest recovery in enterprise networking, and new semicap business,” Ackerman added. “Celestica is poised to benefit from burgeoning data center capex and increasing demand for customized rack-level AI systems and networking switches to support AI workloads.”

data.title: Five9 surges after Q4 beat
data.description:
Shares of Five9 (FIVN) surged about 13% on Friday after fourth quarter results beat estimates.

Q4 Metrics

For the fourth quarter ended Dec. 31, 2025, the company's revenue jumped about 8% year-over-year to $300.28M. Non-GAAP EPS rose about 1.3% year-over-year to $0.80. Both bottom and top line numbers surpassed analysts' estimates.

"We're pleased with our fourth quarter performance, exiting the year with a revenue run rate of $1.2 billion and adjusted EBITDA margin of 26%. The CX market is undergoing a significant transformation as AI becomes central to customer experience," said Chairman Mike Burkland. 

Five9 — which provides cloud software for contact centers — saw ull year 2025 total revenue increase 10% to a record $1.149B, according to the company. 

Outlook

For the first quarter of 2026, Five9 expects revenue between $296.5M and $302.5M (midpoint at $299.5M) versus a consensus revenue estimate of $300.00M. The company expects non-GAAP EPS in the range of $0.66 to $0.70 (midpoint at $0.68) compared to consensus EPS estimate of $0.68.

For the full year 2026, Five9 expects revenue to be in the range of $1.247B to $1.261B (midpoint at $1.254B) versus a consensus revenue estimate of $1.26B. The company expects non-GAAP EPS to be between $3.15 and $3.21 (midpoint at $3.18) compared to a consensus EPS estimate of $3.18.

data.title: Regenxbio dealt win by appeals court in patent spat with Sarepta
data.description:
• A federal appeals court has reversed a lower court ruling that found in favor of Sarepta Therapeutics (SRPT) in a patent dispute with Regenxbio (RGNX).
  • The U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit found that a Delaware federal court decision rendered in January 2024 on the patent was incorrect as claims related to it are "not directed to a natural phenomenon."
  • The case has now been remanded for further proceedings. 
  • The patent in question, 10,526,617 ('617), relates to technology behind Regenxbio's adeno-associated virus (AAV) gene delivery platform. The company claims that Sarepta uses this patent in the manufacture of its Duchenne muscular dystrophy treatment Elevidys (delandistrogene moxeparvovec).
  • Regenxbio has an exclusive license to the '617 patent from the University of Pennsylvania, which is also a plaintiff in the case.

data.title: Dr. Reddy’s biosimilar for Bristol’s Orencia undergoes FDA review
data.description:
Dr. Reddy’s Laboratories (RDY) announced on Friday that the U.S. Food and Drug Administration has accepted for review its marketing application for a biosimilar targeting Orencia, a blockbuster arthritis therapy marketed by Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY).

If approved, the product named DRL_AB will be available as an intravenous infusion for adults with inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis and psoriatic arthritis, the company said.

The Indian drugmaker had submitted the 351 ((k)) Biologics License Application for DRL_AB in December, becoming the first to pursue U.S. approval for an off-patent version of Orencia (abatacept).

The company seeks interchangeable status for the drug, meaning if approved, DRL_AB could be substituted at the pharmacy level without the intervention of the healthcare provider who first prescribed the reference product.

In 2025, Bristol-Myers (BMY) generated $2.7B in revenue from Orencia in the U.S., indicating a ~1% YoY decline as lower selling prices partly offset higher demand.

data.title: Western Union stock drops after Q4 revenue disappoints
data.description:
Western Union (WU) stock fell 6.1% in Friday morning trading after the company's Q4 revenue fell short of consensus, even though its 2026 earnings guidance was largely in line with Wall Street expectations.

The company known for wire transfers of cash expects 2026 adjusted EPS  of $1.75-$1.85 (midpoint $1.80 vs. $1.79 consensus) and full-year revenue growth of 5%-8%, implying $4.25B-$4.37B (midpoint $4.31B vs. the $4.12B consensus).

Q4 adjusted EPS of $0.45, beating the average analyst estimate of $0.43, fell from $0.47 in Q3 and increased from $0.40 in Q4 2024. 

Q4 revenue of $1.01B, trailing the $1.04B consensus, decreased from $1.03B in the prior quarter and $1.06B a year ago. Q4 adjusted revenue, excluding Iraq, was $1.00B in Q4 2025, down from $1.02B in Q3 and $1.03B in Q4 2024.

"Despite a challenging operating environment in 2025, we delivered meaningful progress across the business," said President and CEO Devin McGranahan. "We strengthened our Consumer Services offerings, expanded our owned retail footprint, and accelerated our transition to a more digital-first operating model."

Consumer money transfer revenue of $871.5M fell 7% Y/Y, while consumer services revenue of $136.9M increased 15%. Branded digital revenue, which makes up 30% of consumer money transfer revenue, increased 7% Y/Y on a reported basis.

Western Union's (WU) consumer services business's growth reflects the expansion of its Travel Money business, which included the acquisition of Eurochange Limited, and higher revenues from the company's bill pay business.

data.title: First Solar surges after Supreme Court strikes down Trump's 'reciprocal' tariffs
data.description:
First Solar (FSLR) +5.1% in Friday's trading after the U.S. Supreme Court ruled IEEPA "reciprocal" tariffs are illegal; starting in February 2025, President Trump used the International Emergency Economic Powers Act to implement tariffs on essentially all U.S. imports.

Wells Fargo analysts said First Solar (FSLR) is one of the biggest beneficiaries of the ruling, as the company is helped by lower duties on imported unfinished goods for its U.S. finishing line, which also allows the company to redirect India-made production to the U.S. at higher ASPs; as somewhat of an offset, broader U.S. panel prices could dip slightly, pressuring ASPs.

Wells Fargo said several other clean energy firms should benefit, with as much as 300 bps of margin exposure, including Enphase Energy (ENPH), Nextpower (NXT), Shoals Technologies (SHLS), and Array Technologies (ARRY): shares are up 3.4%, 3%, 2.9%, and 2.1%, respectively.

ETF: (TAN)

data.title: Here are other laws Trump can use to justify tariffs
data.description:
The Supreme Court's decision that it's not within President Donald Trump's authority to impose sweeping tariffs under the International Economic Emergency Powers Act ((IEEPA)) doesn't mean that his ability to impose tariffs will be restricted significantly.

Even before Friday's ruling, administration officials have said they'll look for alternative ways to impose tariffs if the Supreme Court rejected Trump's IEEPA tariffs.

Supreme Court Justice Brett Kavanaugh spells out in his dissent a range of other laws that the administration can turn to to apply such taxes. 

The Trade Act of 1974

Section 122: "permits the President to impose a 'temporary import surcharge' to 'deal with large and serious United States balance-of-payments deficits.'"

Section 201 allows the president to take "appropriate and feasible action," including duties, if the International Trade Commission rules that a specific item is being imported in such large amounts that it causes a “serious injury” or threat to domestic production.

Section 301 allows duties if the president determines "an act, policy, or practice of a foreign country" is "unjustifiable and burdens or restricts United States commerce."

Tariff Act of 1930

Section 338 allows the president to impose tariffs when he determines that "any foreign country places any burden or disadvantage upon the commerce of the United States."

Trade Expansion Act of 1962

Section 232 gives the president the power to "adjust the imports of [an] article and its derivatives so that such imports will not threaten to impair the national security" if the Secretary of Commerce provides a report indicating that need.

According to reporting by Bloomberg News, many of the laws do have limits in terms of amount and their duration. While the Section 232 law has no limits on the tariff rate of duration, the Section 122 regulation puts a 15% cap on the tariff rate and a limit of 150 days, which can be extended with Congressional approval.

Section 201 tariffs have a four-year initial term, which can be extended to a maximum of eight years. Its rate limit is 50%, which must be phased down after a year.

The Section 301 tariffs also have an initial term of four years but can be extended with no maximum limit, Bloomberg said.

"So the Court’s decision is not likely to greatly restrict Presidential tariff authority going forward," Kavanaugh concluded.
